attributes.stem:
A three-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent after accidentally touching a hot iron 30 minutes ago. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date. His parent is visibly distressed and the child is responding positively to his parent.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 3 cm area of erythema and tenderness on his right palm. The skin is dry and intact without blistering. Capillary refill is normal in all digits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Apply ice directly to the burn
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a first-degree burn, characterised by erythema and pain without blistering. The epidermis is affected but remains intact without blisters. This is also known as a superficial epidermal burn. The priority in initial burn management is to stop the burning process and provide appropriate first aid.

Cool running water for 20 minutes is the gold standard initial treatment for thermal burns. This reduces tissue damage, decreases pain, and limits the depth of the burn. The water should be at a temperature of around 15°C. This intervention is effective up to three hours after the burn, and thus is still valid to be performed despite the 30 minutes since the incident occurred.

Ice should never be applied directly to burns as it can cause tissue damage and worsen the injury. The extreme cold can cause vasoconstriction and potentially lead to further tissue damage.

Transfer to burns unit would be excessive for this superficial burn. Burns unit referral is typically reserved for burns that are deep, extensive (>10% total body surface area), or involve special areas like face, hands, or genitals whereas scarring can be problematic. While this burn is on the palm, its superficial nature makes it appropriate for outpatient management.

Bursting blisters is contraindicated as it increases the risk of infection. In this case, there are no blisters as it is a first-degree burn, but even if blisters develop, they should be left intact.

Petroleum jelly should not be applied immediately as it can trap heat in the tissue. After appropriate cooling, simple moisturiser can be used, but the priority is cooling the burn first. Hydrogel products can be used if there is no access to cool clean water too.

After initial management, parent education should include advice about simple analgesia (paracetamol if needed), keeping the area clean and dry, and protecting it from sun exposure. They should be advised to return if there is any sign of infection or if blisters develop. Review of the burn should be done within the first 1 - 2 days as burns can evolve though this less likely with superficial burns.
learningPoints: First-degree burns involve only the epidermis and typically heal within 7 days without scarring.
Cool running water for 20 minutes is the most effective initial treatment for thermal burns.
references:
Cleland, H.  Thermal burns: Assessment and acute management in the general practice setting. Australian Family Physician. 
2012:41(6).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/bf341606-610a-4853-81c6-94eed6476777/Thermal-burns-assessment-and-acute-management.aspx

Alfred Health & VicBurns. Severe electrical burns. Victorian Adult Burns Service. 2024. Available at: https://www.vicburns.org.au/severe-burns/secondary-survey/severe-electrical-burns/#:~:text=Electr...'s%20Major%20Trauma%20criteria
